title: On the gradient-flow structure of multiphase mean curvature flow
speaker: Tim Laux
abstract: Due to its importance in materials science where it models the slow relaxation of grain boundaries, multiphase mean curvature flow has received a lot of attention over the last decades.

proof_stages.label: Multiphase mean-curvature flow and phase-field gradient flows
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up hypersurface mean-curvature flow, its multiphase partition version with surface tensions, mobilities, and triple-junction force balance, then recalls its formal gradient-flow energy/metric structure and the Allen-Cahn phase-field approximation.

proof_stages.label: Conditional convergence theorem and weak formulation
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the finite-well vector-valued setting, the speaker states compactness of well-prepared Allen-Cahn solutions and the conditional theorem that energy convergence implies convergence to a distributional multiphase mean-curvature flow, with normal velocities and a variational equation encoding both curvature motion and junction balance.

proof_stages.label: Tilt-excess mechanism for passing to the limit
proof_stages.summary: The proof is motivated by minimizing-movement and thresholding analogues and replaces an energy-excess/regularity argument by an epsilon-level tilt excess that controls the weak-times-weak term obtained when testing Allen-Cahn with a vector-field derivative.

proof_stages.label: Energy-convergence consequences and vector-valued rank-one replacement
proof_stages.summary: Energy convergence is used to identify the mixed gradient-potential term and remove discrepancy in the scalar case, while in the vector-valued multiphase case the rescaled gradient is controlled instead by a rank-one structure involving geodesic tangents in well space and the physical interface normal.

proof_stages.label: Weak-strong uniqueness theorem and singularity caveat
proof_stages.summary: The second result states that in two space dimensions a weak solution with optimal energy dissipation and the same initial data as a smooth regular network must agree with it, while nonuniqueness examples show why the conclusion can only hold before singularities.

proof_stages.label: Relative-entropy inequality from the weak solution
proof_stages.summary: The relative entropy is chosen as the same tilt-excess functional evaluated on the weak interfaces against calibration fields from the strong solution, and differentiating it uses energy dissipation, the weak transport identity, and the distributional curvature equation to reduce stability to a list of calibration estimates.

proof_stages.label: Calibration wish list for the strong network
proof_stages.summary: The required strong-solution data are formulated as vector fields extending the interface normals and a velocity field transporting them, with divergence and transport errors small to first or second order in the signed distance to the strong interfaces.

proof_stages.label: Construction of velocity and normal calibrations near triple junctions
proof_stages.summary: Away from junctions one uses signed-distance normal extensions, while near a 120-degree triple junction one chooses compatible tangential velocities, interpolates the velocity field, and builds normal extensions by rotating the neighboring interface constructions so that the calibration conditions remain regular across the junction.

examples.name: Shrinking sphere and neck pinch
examples.note: The shrinking sphere fixes the sign convention for mean curvature flow, while the thin-neck picture illustrates possible topology change under the same evolution.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites spheres shrinking and a thin neck disappearing as standard behaviors fixing intuition and sign, without computing or analyzing them.

examples.name: Polycrystalline grain growth
examples.note: The phases are interpreted as crystal grains separated by migrating grain boundaries, motivating the multiphase mean-curvature model as a materials-science law.
examples.engagement_reason: Polycrystalline grain growth is described as the motivating material-science setting for the equations, but no particular grain configuration is worked through.

passes.reason: Polycrystalline grain growth is presented as the standard physical application of multiphase mean-curvature flow.

examples.name: Two-phase vector-valued diffuse interface with non-straight well geodesic
examples.note: A single physical interface whose phase-field values travel along a non-straight geodesic between vector wells shows why the full rescaled gradient may oscillate and why one must replace scalar normal convergence by a rank-one tangent-times-normal structure.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes a two-phase vector-valued interface between wells, traces the transition along a non-straight geodesic in well space, and derives the resulting oscillatory/rank-one structure of the rescaled gradient.

passes.reason: A non-straight well geodesic is a generic vector-valued interface scenario used to show failure of strong convergence of the full gradient.
A vector-valued two-well diffuse interface with a curved well-geodesic is used to show that the full rescaled gradient need not converge strongly.
The minimal two-phase vector-valued setup with a curved well geodesic is used to show full-gradient strong convergence can fail.
A vector-valued diffuse interface following a non-straight well geodesic is used to show scalar-style strong convergence of the full gradient can fail.

examples.name: Taylor four-sector nonuniqueness after a vanishing grain
examples.note: The symmetric cross can split into two different pairs of triple junctions, and the shrinking central grain shows how such nonuniqueness can arise only after a singularity.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws and reasons through Taylor’s four-sector scenario, explaining the two possible triple-junction splittings and how a shrinking middle grain can lead to that nonunique configuration.

passes.reason: The vanished central grain creates a symmetric collapsed four-sector singularity, used to demonstrate nonuniqueness beyond singular times.
